Surname.admissibility_guide: The applicant must be identifiable as a specific individual. Applications attributed to no identified person are treated as anonymous and declared inadmissible under Article 35 § 2(a). Rule 47 requires full identification of every applicant.
Surname.application_notes: Write your family name exactly as it appears in your official identity document (passport or national ID card). Use the standard form used in official documents in your country.
Surname.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 1: Not using the Court’s current application form. If the form is outdated, the name fields may not link correctly to the Court’s database system. Always download the latest form from www.echr.coe.int.
Surname.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

First name(s).admissibility_guide: All given names are required to identify the applicant unambiguously. The Court maintains a database of applications and accurate identification prevents filing errors that could affect the processing of the four-month deadline.
First name(s).application_notes: Enter all first names as they appear in your official identity document. Do not use abbreviations or initials only. If you have multiple given names, write all of them.
First name(s).common_mistakes: Writing only initials or a single name where the applicant has multiple given names can cause identification issues and database mismatches. Use the full name as in the passport.
First name(s).case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Date of birth.admissibility_guide: Date of birth is an essential identifier distinguishing applicants with the same name. It may also be relevant to victim status (e.g. cases involving minors, age discrimination, or rights that vary by age).
Date of birth.application_notes: Format: DD/MM/YYYY (e.g. 31/12/1960). The form provides individual digit boxes for day, month and year. Do not use alternative formats such as MM/DD/YYYY.
Date of birth.common_mistakes: Incorrect date format or a missing date of birth causes data entry errors in the Court’s database. This is a basic identification field — it must be completed precisely.
Date of birth.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Place of birth.admissibility_guide: Place of birth confirms identity and may be relevant to establishing the applicant’s nationality, the respondent State’s jurisdiction, and any connection to the events complained of.
Place of birth.application_notes: Write the town or city of birth and the country. If the country has since changed its name or borders, write the name at the time of birth, followed in brackets by the current country name (e.g. Lviv, Ukrainian SSR (now Ukraine)).
Place of birth.common_mistakes: Writing only a country name is insufficient. Provide the specific town or city as well as the country. If the place of birth is unknown, state this explicitly.
Place of birth.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Nationality.admissibility_guide: Nationality is relevant to admissibility. The Court can only examine complaints against States that have ratified the Convention or the relevant Protocol. Nationality also bears on jurisdiction ratione personae. Stateless persons should write “stateless”.
Nationality.application_notes: Write your current nationality as in your travel document. If you hold dual or multiple nationalities, state all of them. Stateless persons should write “stateless”.
Nationality.common_mistakes: Stating only a former nationality, or omitting dual nationality, can create confusion about the respondent State’s obligations and the applicant’s connection to the Convention system.
Nationality.case_law:
Banković and Others v. Belgium and Others  ECtHR (Grand Chamber, dec.), 12 December 2001
Jurisdiction under the Convention is primarily territorial. Nationality alone does not establish the Court’s jurisdiction: the key question is whether the alleged violation occurred within the jurisdiction of a Convention State. However, the applicant’s nationality remains a required identifying detail.

Address.admissibility_guide: The Court must be able to correspond directly with the applicant. If no representative is appointed, or if the representative later withdraws, the Court will write to this address. A file that receives no reply becomes inactive and may be closed after six months.
Address.application_notes: Provide a full postal address separate from any lawyer’s address. If homeless or without a fixed address, provide a friend’s address, P.O. box or other contact address, with an explanation. Notify the Court immediately if your address changes.
Address.common_mistakes: Using only the lawyer’s address for the applicant is not acceptable. The Court needs an independent contact address for the applicant in case the representation arrangement ends.
Address.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Telephone.admissibility_guide: Contact details are a formal requirement under Rule 47. A working telephone number allows the Registry to reach the applicant quickly for time-sensitive communications, particularly if a representative has not yet been appointed.
Telephone.application_notes: Include the full international dialling code (e.g. +49 30 12345678 for Germany). Write the number in a standard international format. Indicate if the number is mobile or landline.
Telephone.common_mistakes: Providing a number without the international dialling code, or a number that is no longer in use, prevents the Registry from contacting you by phone.
Telephone.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Email.admissibility_guide: An email address provides the Court with an additional means of contact. It is labelled “if any” and is not mandatory if the applicant does not have one. However, if provided, it must be actively monitored.
Email.application_notes: Provide a working email address if you have one. This field is optional. If provided, check it regularly. Inform the Court if your email address changes.
Email.common_mistakes: Providing an inactive or incorrect email address may result in missed communications. If you change your email address after lodging the application, notify the Court promptly.
Email.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Sex.admissibility_guide: Sex is an identification detail required under Rule 47. It may also be directly relevant where the complaint involves differential treatment on grounds of sex under Article 14 (prohibition of discrimination).
Sex.application_notes: Tick the appropriate box: male or female. This is a required identification field.
Sex.common_mistakes: Leaving the sex box unticked creates an incomplete record in the Court’s database. It is a simple administrative requirement that must not be overlooked.
Sex.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Name.admissibility_guide: Under Article 34, applications may be lodged by “any non-governmental organisation or group of individuals”. The organisation’s name must be stated precisely as registered so that its legal existence and standing can be verified.
Name.application_notes: Write the full official name of the organisation as it appears in the official register. Do not abbreviate unless the abbreviation is the registered name itself.
Name.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 6: Where organisations do not fill in their identifying details. The organisation’s name and the details of its official representative in Section D.1 must both be complete.
Name.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Identification number.admissibility_guide: The official registration number enables the Court to verify the legal existence of the organisation. Unregistered entities may still apply but must explain their legal status.
Identification number.application_notes: Provide the official identification or registration number assigned in the official register, if any. If the organisation has no such number, leave this field blank and explain the organisation’s legal basis in the form.
Identification number.common_mistakes: Omitting the identification number when one exists may raise questions about the organisation’s legal standing. Always include it where available.
Identification number.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Date of registration.admissibility_guide: The date of registration establishes when the organisation came into existence as a legal entity. This may be relevant to determining whether the alleged violation post-dates the organisation’s founding and whether it has standing to complain.
Date of registration.application_notes: Format: DD/MM/YYYY. Include the date of formal registration, formation or incorporation where such a procedure was followed in your country. Labelled “if any” — leave blank if no formal registration procedure applies.
Date of registration.common_mistakes: Leaving this blank when the date is known makes it harder to verify the organisation’s legal status and may slow processing of the application.
Date of registration.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Activity.admissibility_guide: The nature of the organisation’s activities is relevant to assessing victim status. An NGO can generally only bring a complaint on behalf of those directly affected by the alleged violation, not as a general public interest litigant, unless domestic law explicitly provides for such standing.
Activity.application_notes: Describe the main purpose or activity briefly (e.g. “trade union representing construction workers” or “non-governmental organisation defending press freedom”).
Activity.common_mistakes: A vague or missing description makes it harder for the Court to assess victim status and standing. Be specific about what the organisation does and who it represents.
Activity.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Registered address.admissibility_guide: The registered address is necessary for the Court’s correspondence and for verifying the organisation’s legal domicile. It may also be relevant to establishing connection to the respondent State.
Registered address.application_notes: Provide the full official registered address as it appears in the official register: street, city and country. If the operational address differs from the registered address, note both.
Registered address.common_mistakes: Providing a postal address that differs from the registered address without explanation may create confusion. Include the registered address specifically, even if correspondence goes elsewhere.
Registered address.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Telephone.admissibility_guide: Contact information is a formal requirement under Rule 47. An organisation must ensure the Court can reach it through its official representative.
Telephone.application_notes: Include the full international dialling code. An organisational telephone number is preferable to a personal mobile, but either is acceptable if it reliably reaches the official representative.
Telephone.common_mistakes: If the person responsible for the application changes, ensure the telephone contact is updated with the Court to avoid missed communications.
Telephone.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Email.admissibility_guide: Email is part of the formal contact information required under Rule 47. Once the case reaches the notification stage, the eComms system (Field 57) will be the primary electronic communication channel.
Email.application_notes: Use an institutional email address (e.g. info@organisation.org) rather than a personal account. This ensures continuity of access if individual staff members change.
Email.common_mistakes: Using a personal email address for the organisation is not ideal. If the responsible individual leaves the organisation, the Court must be notified of the new contact.
Email.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

B. States.admissibility_guide:
The Court’s competence depends entirely on the respondent State being identified and having ratified the Convention. Complaints can only be directed against one of the listed Contracting States. The Court cannot examine complaints against States outside the Convention system, private individuals, or international organisations.

The application must be directed against the State responsible for the alleged violation — not necessarily the applicant’s State of nationality. The key question is whether the act or omission is attributable to the respondent State.
B. States.application_notes:
Tick the box of the Contracting State(s) responsible for the violations you are complaining about. You may tick more than one State if the complaints relate to different countries.

Note: Russia (RUS) ceased to be a party to the Convention on 16 September 2022 and cannot be a respondent State for events after that date.
B. States.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 9: Forgetting to tick the country box. The Court’s competence depends on the respondent State being identified. The Court cannot guess which State is responsible. At least one box must be ticked for the application to be complete.
B. States.case_law:
Banković and Others v. Belgium and Others  ECtHR (Grand Chamber, dec.), 12 December 2001
Jurisdiction under the Convention is primarily territorial. A Contracting State’s jurisdiction normally covers its own territory; extraterritorial application is exceptional. Field 17 must identify the State that had jurisdiction over the act or omission complained of.

Vučković and Others v. Serbia  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 25 March 2014
The respondent State in Field 17 must be consistent with the State whose courts were used for domestic remedies in Field 63. Identifying a different respondent State from the one against which you exhausted remedies creates an admissibility problem.

Capacity / relationship / function.admissibility_guide: The legal basis on which a person acts on the applicant’s behalf must be clearly established. Without it, the Court cannot verify the representative’s authority. An unverified authority means the Registry will correspond with the applicant directly.
Capacity / relationship / function.application_notes: Describe the capacity clearly: e.g. “mother of applicant”, “legal guardian appointed by [court] on [date]”, “trustee under power of attorney dated [date]”. Attach documentary proof where available.
Capacity / relationship / function.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 11: Sending a separate authority form instead of completing Section C.3. The representative’s capacity and identity must be on the form itself, with original signatures in Fields 33 and 35.
Capacity / relationship / function.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Surname.admissibility_guide: The full identifying details of the representative are a formal requirement under Rule 47. The Court will correspond primarily with the representative once appointed.
Surname.application_notes: Write the representative’s family name as in their official identity document.
Surname.common_mistakes: Incomplete name details prevent the Registry from identifying and corresponding with the representative.
Surname.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

First name(s).admissibility_guide: All given names must be provided for the representative. This avoids confusion where different individuals share the same surname.
First name(s).application_notes: Write all first names as in the official identity document. Do not abbreviate.
First name(s).common_mistakes: Partial names create identification problems. Write the full name.
First name(s).case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Nationality.admissibility_guide: The representative’s nationality is part of their identification. It may also be relevant where the representative themselves is subject to any conflict of interest with the respondent State.
Nationality.application_notes: Write the representative’s current nationality as in their official identity document.
Nationality.common_mistakes: Omitting nationality makes the representative’s identification incomplete.
Nationality.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Address.admissibility_guide: The representative’s postal address is the primary correspondence address once they are appointed. If this address is wrong or inactive, communications from the Court will not be received and the file may become inactive.
Address.application_notes: Provide a full postal address for correspondence: street, city, postcode and country. If the representative’s address changes, notify the Court immediately.
Address.common_mistakes: A missing or incorrect address means correspondence cannot reach the representative. An inactive file may be closed after six months.
Address.case_law:
Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine  ECtHR (admissibility decision)
Incomplete formal information results in the application not being registered. If representative contact details are missing or wrong, correspondence cannot be delivered — leading to the file being closed as inactive.

Telephone.admissibility_guide: Telephone contact details allow the Registry to reach the representative quickly for urgent or time-sensitive communications.
Telephone.application_notes: Include the full international dialling code. Indicate whether the number is mobile or landline.
Telephone.common_mistakes: An outdated or missing telephone number means the Registry cannot contact the representative urgently. Keep it current.
Telephone.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Fax.admissibility_guide: A fax number is optional but useful for some Registry communications. Note that faxed applications are not accepted as a means of lodging the application itself — only original postal submissions are valid.
Fax.application_notes: Provide the fax number with full international dialling code, if available. If you do not have a fax, leave this field blank.
Fax.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 5: Do not attempt to lodge the application by fax. A faxed application is not counted as a complete application. The paper form with original signatures must be sent by post.
Fax.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Email.admissibility_guide: Email provides an additional contact channel. The eComms system is not linked to the non-lawyer representative’s email at Field 25; it is the lawyer’s email at Field 37 that is used for eComms.
Email.application_notes: Provide a working email address. This field is for general correspondence purposes.
Email.common_mistakes: Providing an inactive email address may result in missed communications from the Registry.
Email.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Surname.admissibility_guide: The lawyer’s full identity is a formal requirement under Rule 47. Once the lawyer is identified and signs in Field 35, the Registry will correspond only with them as the authorised representative.
Surname.application_notes: Write the lawyer’s family name as in professional records or the bar register.
Surname.common_mistakes: Incomplete lawyer identification means the Registry cannot process the appointment. The Registry will continue corresponding with the applicant until the lawyer’s details are complete and signed.
Surname.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

First name(s).admissibility_guide: All given names of the lawyer are required for unambiguous identification in the Court’s records.
First name(s).application_notes: Write all first names as in the lawyer’s professional registration. Do not abbreviate.
First name(s).common_mistakes: Partial names may cause confusion where multiple lawyers at the same firm share a surname.
First name(s).case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Nationality.admissibility_guide: The lawyer’s nationality is part of their formal identification required under Rule 47.
Nationality.application_notes: State the lawyer’s current nationality as in their official documentation.
Nationality.common_mistakes: Omitting the lawyer’s nationality creates an incomplete identification record.
Nationality.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Address.admissibility_guide: The lawyer’s professional address becomes the primary correspondence address. All Registry letters, notifications and decisions will be sent here. An incorrect or outdated address means correspondence is lost.
Address.application_notes: Provide the law firm’s or chambers’ professional postal address: street, city, postcode and country. Keep this address current and notify the Court of any changes.
Address.common_mistakes: Using a home address instead of a professional address is permissible but less appropriate. Whatever address is given must be actively monitored and kept current.
Address.case_law:
Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine  ECtHR (admissibility decision)
Incomplete contact details for the representative can lead to missed correspondence and the file being treated as inactive. Once a file is closed as inactive, it cannot simply be reopened.

Telephone.admissibility_guide: A working telephone number for the lawyer allows the Registry to make urgent contact. This is especially important when the Court requires a fast response.
Telephone.application_notes: Include the full international dialling code. Provide the lawyer’s direct professional number.
Telephone.common_mistakes: A missing or incorrect telephone number means the Registry cannot reach the lawyer urgently. Keep contact details current at all times.
Telephone.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Fax.admissibility_guide: A fax number is optional but useful for certain Registry transmissions. As with Field 24, fax cannot be used to lodge the application itself.
Fax.application_notes: Provide a professional fax number with full international dialling code, if available. Leave blank if no fax is used.
Fax.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 5: A faxed application form does not count as a lodged application. The original signed paper form must be sent by post. Fax details here are only for subsequent correspondence.
Fax.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Email.admissibility_guide: The lawyer’s email at Field 32 is linked to the eComms account at Field 37. eComms becomes the primary electronic communication channel once the Government is given notice of the application.
Email.application_notes: Provide a professional email address. This should match or be consistent with the eComms email at Field 37. A generic firm email (e.g. echr@lawfirm.com) is recommended so that multiple fee-earners can access correspondence.
Email.common_mistakes: Using a personal email address creates risk of loss of access if the responsible lawyer leaves the firm. Use a shared institutional address.
Email.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Signature of applicant.admissibility_guide: The applicant’s signature on the authority is a mandatory formal requirement under Rule 47. It gives the application legal effect and confirms that the representative has been authorised. Without it, the application is not a valid legal document.
Signature of applicant.application_notes: Original signature required. If the applicant is a child or lacks legal capacity, the non-lawyer representative signs on their behalf. Sign in ink — not in pencil.
Signature of applicant.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 5: Not sending the application with the original signature. A photocopy, scan or fax of the signature is not accepted. The physically signed paper form must be sent by post.
Signature of applicant.case_law:
Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine  ECtHR (admissibility decision)
An incomplete application — including one with defective or missing signatures — is not registered and does not stop the four-month clock. The signature is not a formality: it gives the application legal effect.

Date of applicant’s signature.admissibility_guide: The date of the signature is relevant to the admissibility timeline. It should be consistent with the date of dispatch of the form.
Date of applicant’s signature.application_notes: Format: DD/MM/YYYY. Sign and date the form on or close to the day it is sent. Do not pre-date or post-date.
Date of applicant’s signature.common_mistakes: Leaving the date blank or using a date significantly different from the date of dispatch may prompt queries from the Registry and create uncertainty about the validity of the authority.
Date of applicant’s signature.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Signature of representative.admissibility_guide: The representative’s signature confirms their acceptance of the commission to act. Without this signature, the Registry has no proof that the representative is actually involved in the case and may correspond only with the applicant.
Signature of representative.application_notes: Original signature required. The lawyer or other person listed as representative in C.1 or C.2 must sign here. Both applicant (Field 33) and representative (Field 35) must sign.
Signature of representative.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 11: Both signatures must be on the application form itself, not on a separate authority form. A separate authority is only accepted where there are insurmountable practical obstacles to signing the form.
Signature of representative.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Date of representative’s signature.admissibility_guide: The date of the representative’s signature confirms when the commission was accepted. Both Fields 34 and 36 should show dates consistent with the date of dispatch.
Date of representative’s signature.application_notes: Format: DD/MM/YYYY. Both the applicant and the representative should sign and date on or close to the same day.
Date of representative’s signature.common_mistakes: A signed but undated authority creates uncertainty about when the representation became effective. Both Fields 34 and 36 must be completed.
Date of representative’s signature.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Email address for eComms account (indivi.admissibility_guide: eComms is the Court’s secure electronic communication portal for representatives. It is activated only when the Government is given notice of the application. At the initial lodging stage, all communication is by post.
Email address for eComms account (indivi.application_notes: Use a generic institutional email address (e.g. echr@lawfirm.com) so the account can be accessed by multiple colleagues within the firm. Notify the Court of any change to the eComms email. See: https://ecomms.echr.coe.int
Email address for eComms account (indivi.common_mistakes: Using a personal email address creates risk of loss of access if the responsible lawyer leaves the firm. Use a shared institutional address and update it if circumstances change.
Email address for eComms account (indivi.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Capacity / relationship / function (plea.admissibility_guide: An organisation can only act through a person legally authorised to represent it. The Court must verify this chain of authority. Without documentary proof, the application may be rejected for lack of proper authorisation.
Capacity / relationship / function (plea.application_notes: State the official’s capacity clearly: e.g. “Chief Executive Officer”, “Chairperson”, “Director”. Attach proof of authorisation: extract from the company register, notarised authorisation, or minutes of the governing body.
Capacity / relationship / function (plea.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 6: Even where an organisation has a lawyer, the organisation official’s details must be completed in D.1. Copies of the minutes, articles of association, or other proof of authorisation must be attached.
Capacity / relationship / function (plea.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Surname.admissibility_guide: The full name of the authorised official is required for the Court’s records and so that the official can be identified as the person who signs the authority in Field 53.
Surname.application_notes: Write the official’s family name as in their identity document.
Surname.common_mistakes: Incomplete name details for the organisation official create gaps in the chain of authority and prevent proper identification.
Surname.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

First name(s).admissibility_guide: All given names are required for unambiguous identification of the authorised official.
First name(s).application_notes: Write all first names as in the official’s identity document. Do not abbreviate.
First name(s).common_mistakes: Partial names may cause confusion, particularly in organisations with multiple staff members. Write the full name.
First name(s).case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Nationality.admissibility_guide: The official’s nationality is part of their formal identification required under Rule 47.
Nationality.application_notes: Write the official’s current nationality as in their official documentation.
Nationality.common_mistakes: Omitting nationality makes the official’s identification record incomplete.
Nationality.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Address.admissibility_guide: The official’s address enables the Court to correspond with the organisation’s authorised representative directly. If the organisation appoints a lawyer, correspondence will go to the lawyer; otherwise, it goes to the official at this address.
Address.application_notes: Provide a full postal contact address: street, city, postcode and country. Notify the Court if this address changes.
Address.common_mistakes: An incorrect or inactive address means Registry correspondence will not reach the organisation. Keep this address current.
Address.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Telephone.admissibility_guide: Telephone contact for the official enables urgent Registry communications to reach the organisation.
Telephone.application_notes: Include the full international dialling code.
Telephone.common_mistakes: Outdated or missing telephone details prevent urgent contact. Keep this information current.
Telephone.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Fax.admissibility_guide: An optional contact detail. A fax number cannot be used to lodge the application itself.
Fax.application_notes: Provide the fax number with full international dialling code if available. Leave blank if not used.
Fax.common_mistakes: As with all fax fields: fax cannot substitute for an original signed postal submission.
Fax.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Email.admissibility_guide: Email contact for the organisation official provides an additional channel. The eComms system (Field 57) will be the main electronic communication channel for the lawyer once the application is notified.
Email.application_notes: Provide a working professional email address. Use an institutional address rather than a personal one where possible.
Email.common_mistakes: A personal email address risks loss of access if the official changes role. An institutional address linked to the organisation is preferable.
Email.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Surname.admissibility_guide: Full identification of the lawyer representing the organisation is required under Rule 47. Once listed and signed, the lawyer becomes the Court’s primary correspondent.
Surname.application_notes: Write the lawyer’s family name as in professional records or the bar register.
Surname.common_mistakes: Incomplete lawyer details mean the Registry cannot process the appointment. It will continue corresponding with the organisation official until the lawyer’s details are complete and signed.
Surname.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

First name(s).admissibility_guide: All given names are required for unambiguous identification in the Court’s records.
First name(s).application_notes: Write all first names as in the lawyer’s professional registration. Do not abbreviate.
First name(s).common_mistakes: Partial names may cause confusion. Write the full name.
First name(s).case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Nationality.admissibility_guide: Nationality is part of the formal identification of the lawyer required under Rule 47.
Nationality.common_mistakes: Omitting the lawyer’s nationality creates an incomplete identification record.
Nationality.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Address.admissibility_guide: The lawyer’s professional address becomes the primary correspondence address for the organisation’s application. All Registry communications will be directed here.
Address.application_notes: Provide the law firm’s or chambers’ postal address. Notify the Court of any changes.
Address.common_mistakes: An incorrect or outdated address means correspondence is lost. Failure to respond to Registry correspondence can result in the file being closed.
Address.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Telephone.admissibility_guide: A working telephone number for the lawyer allows the Registry to make urgent contact.
Telephone.application_notes: Include the full international dialling code. Provide the lawyer’s direct professional number.
Telephone.common_mistakes: A missing or outdated telephone number means the Registry cannot reach the lawyer urgently.
Telephone.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Fax.admissibility_guide: Optional. A fax number is only for subsequent correspondence; it cannot be used to lodge the application.
Fax.application_notes: Provide with full international dialling code if available. Leave blank if unused.
Fax.common_mistakes: Fax cannot substitute for the original signed postal submission.
Fax.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Email.admissibility_guide: The lawyer’s email at Field 52 is linked to the eComms account at Field 57. This becomes the primary electronic communication channel once the application is notified to the Government.
Email.application_notes: Use a professional or generic firm email address. This should match or be consistent with the email at Field 57. A shared firm address is recommended.
Email.common_mistakes: A personal email address risks inaccessibility if the responsible lawyer changes. Use a shared institutional address.
Email.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Signature of organisation official.admissibility_guide: The organisation official’s signature empowers the lawyer to act. Without it, the application is not a valid legal document. It establishes the chain of authority from the organisation through its official to the lawyer.
Signature of organisation official.application_notes: Original signature required. The person signing must be the same person identified in Section D.1 (Field 38–45). This signature mirrors Field 33 for individual applicants.
Signature of organisation official.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 5 and 11: Original signatures only; no photocopies; both sections D.1 and D.3 must be completed together.
Signature of organisation official.case_law:
Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine  ECtHR (admissibility decision)
An application is only registered when complete, including properly executed authority sections. A missing signature means the application is not registered and the four-month clock is not interrupted.

Date of organisation official’s signatur.admissibility_guide: The date of the official’s signature confirms when the authority was granted and should be consistent with the date of dispatch.
Date of organisation official’s signatur.application_notes: Format: DD/MM/YYYY. Sign and date on or close to the date the form is sent.
Date of organisation official’s signatur.common_mistakes: A signed but undated authority creates uncertainty. Field 54 must be completed alongside Field 53.
Date of organisation official’s signatur.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Signature of lawyer (for organisation).admissibility_guide: The lawyer’s signature confirms acceptance of the commission to act for the organisation. Without this signature the Registry cannot treat the lawyer as the authorised representative and will continue corresponding only with the organisation official.
Signature of lawyer (for organisation).application_notes: Original signature required. The lawyer identified in Section D.2 must sign here, confirming they have accepted the commission.
Signature of lawyer (for organisation).common_mistakes: Mistake no. 11: Both the organisation official and the lawyer must sign the authority on the application form itself at the time of preparation, not on a separate document.
Signature of lawyer (for organisation).case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Date of lawyer’s signature (for organisa.admissibility_guide: The date confirms when the lawyer accepted the commission. Fields 54 and 56 should show dates consistent with the date of dispatch.
Date of lawyer’s signature (for organisa.application_notes: Format: DD/MM/YYYY. Both signatures (Fields 53 and 55) and both dates (Fields 54 and 56) must be present for the authority to be valid.
Date of lawyer’s signature (for organisa.common_mistakes: A signed but undated authority is incomplete. Both Fields 54 and 56 must be filled in.
Date of lawyer’s signature (for organisa.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Email address for eComms account (organi.admissibility_guide: eComms is activated when the Government is given notice of the application. Until then, all communication is by post. The email at Field 57 is linked to the eComms account for the organisation’s representative.
Email address for eComms account (organi.application_notes: Use a generic institutional email address (not a personal account) to allow shared access within the legal team. Notify the Court of any change. See: https://ecomms.echr.coe.int
Email address for eComms account (organi.common_mistakes: A personal email address creates risk of access loss if the responsible lawyer changes role. Use a shared institutional address.
Email address for eComms account (organi.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Statement of the Facts — Page 1.admissibility_guide:
Rule 47 requires “a concise and legible statement of the facts” sufficient for the Court to determine the nature and scope of the application without reference to any other document.

Proceedings before the Court are adversarial: it is for you to substantiate your factual arguments with evidence. Applications risk being declared manifestly ill-founded where the factual basis is not properly established.
Statement of the Facts — Page 1.application_notes:
Be clear, concise and chronological. Give exact dates.

•	Start with the event or official decision at the heart of your complaint.
•	Use precise dates (e.g. 05/03/2019 — not “early 2019”).
•	Name the authorities, courts and officials involved and their actions.
•	If your case involves several different matters, deal with each separately.
•	Attach supporting documents: court decisions, medical reports, eviction notices, etc.
•	If a document cannot be obtained, explain in the form why.
Statement of the Facts — Page 1.common_mistakes:
Mistake no. 2: Not putting a summary on the form. A complete account — with facts, alleged violations and remedies — must appear directly on the form. Leaving the facts pages blank and referring to attachments is not accepted.

Mistake no. 3: Not attaching the decision complained of. A translation alone does not replace the original document.
Statement of the Facts — Page 1.case_law:
Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine  ECtHR (admissibility decision)
Applications rejected because copies of domestic decisions and appeal documents were missing. Without these, exhaustion of remedies cannot be verified. An incomplete application does not stop the four-month clock.

Klass and Others v. Germany  ECtHR, 6 September 1978
Applications must show a prima facie (arguable) claim of a violation. A complaint unsupported by a plausible factual basis will be rejected as manifestly ill-founded. Field 58 must contain a concrete, evidence-backed account.

Statement of the Facts (continued) — Pag.admissibility_guide: Continue the factual account. The Court needs the full procedural history to assess whether the Convention was violated. Incomplete or fragmented accounts risk the application being declared inadmissible as unsubstantiated.
Statement of the Facts (continued) — Pag.application_notes:
Continue in strict chronological order, covering procedural steps: hearings, appeals, administrative decisions and their outcomes.

•	Describe each relevant court or authority decision: name, date, outcome.
•	Include all significant procedural steps.
•	Keep the language factual — save legal arguments for Part F.
Statement of the Facts (continued) — Pag.common_mistakes:
Mistake no. 2 (continued): Starting the facts on the form and continuing on attached sheets is not accepted. The Court must understand the key facts from the form alone.

Mistake no. 4: Not providing copies of all court decisions at every level, and copies of your grounds of appeal.
Statement of the Facts (continued) — Pag.case_law:
Vučković and Others v. Serbia  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 25 March 2014
The obligation to exhaust domestic remedies requires the full procedural history to be on record. Courts cannot piece together a case from scattered submissions.

Statement of the Facts (continued) — Pag.admissibility_guide: This final page is where the Court looks to establish the starting point of the four-month time-limit. The period normally runs from the date the highest competent court gave its decision, or the date it was served. The Court applies this rule of its own motion.
Statement of the Facts (continued) — Pag.application_notes:
•	State the outcome of the highest court or authority.
•	Give the exact date of the final decision AND the date you received it.
•	Note any significant facts following the final decision.
•	Do not exceed the space provided — the complete factual picture must fit within Fields 58–60.

Privacy: Include private details only as strictly necessary for the case. Information may become publicly accessible via HUDOC.
Statement of the Facts (continued) — Pag.common_mistakes:
Mistake no. 2 (continued): An annex (max. 20 pages) may only develop what is on the form — it cannot replace the factual account.

Mistake no. 12: Sending the form at the last moment before the deadline. If something is missing you will have no time to resubmit.
Statement of the Facts (continued) — Pag.case_law:
Sabri Güneş v. Turkey  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 29 June 2012
The four-month time-limit begins from the date of the final domestic decision. Field 60 must clearly establish when the final decision was given and when it was received.

Otto v. Germany / Dennis and Others v. United Kingdom  ECtHR (admissibility decisions)
The time-limit begins when the applicant knew or ought to have known of the final domestic decision. Where notification and the decision date differ, this must be documented.

Article invoked + Explanation — Page 1.admissibility_guide:
A complaint will be declared manifestly ill-founded if a preliminary examination does not disclose any “appearance of a violation” of a Convention right.

Rule 47 requires “a concise and legible statement of the alleged violation(s) and the relevant arguments.” Both the Article and a brief explanation are mandatory for each complaint.

The fourth-instance principle: The Court is not an appeal court from national courts and cannot annul or alter their decisions. A complaint that merely challenges the outcome of a national court — without pointing to a specific Convention violation — will be rejected.
Article invoked + Explanation — Page 1.application_notes:
•	Left column: The Article of the Convention (or Protocol) invoked.
•	Right column: Brief explanation of how that Article was violated, connected to the facts in Part E.
•	Give a separate explanation for each individual complaint.

Example: Article 6 § 1: The civil proceedings lasted over ten years, from 10 January 2002 to 25 April 2012, constituting an unreasonable length of time.

Common Articles: Art. 2 (life), Art. 3 (torture/inhuman treatment), Art. 5 (liberty), Art. 6 (fair trial), Art. 8 (private/family life), Art. 10 (expression), Art. 14 (non-discrimination).
Article invoked + Explanation — Page 1.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 7: Not filling in the Statement of Violations. The Court cannot infer from the facts what the applicant considers to be a breach. Both the Article and the explanation are required for every complaint.
Article invoked + Explanation — Page 1.case_law:
Klass and Others v. Germany  ECtHR, 6 September 1978
Applications must show a prima facie arguable violation. Field 61 must be specific enough for the Court to identify a credible Convention issue.

Golder v. United Kingdom  ECtHR, 21 February 1975
Article 6(1) necessarily includes the right of access to a court. If your complaint concerns denial of access to proceedings, state this explicitly. Hindrance in fact violates the Convention just as a formal legal bar.

Piersack v. Belgium  ECtHR, 1 October 1982
Impartiality can be tested subjectively (personal bias) and objectively (legitimate doubt). If invoking Article 6 on impartiality, address both limbs. Proof of actual bias is not required.

Kudła v. Poland  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 26 October 2000
If invoking Article 6(1) on length of proceedings, also consider Article 13: states must provide an effective domestic remedy for complaints about excessive length. If such a remedy existed and was not used, this must be explained.

Article invoked + Explanation (continued.admissibility_guide: Continue listing complaints. Each must independently disclose an appearance of a violation. The Court assesses each complaint separately; a finding on one does not affect admissibility of others.
Article invoked + Explanation (continued.application_notes:
•	Continue with remaining complaints using the same two-column structure.
•	Do not exceed the space provided on the form.
•	An optional annex (max. 20 pages total) may develop complaints further, but no new complaints can be introduced in the annex.
Article invoked + Explanation (continued.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 7 (continued): Both columns must be filled in for every complaint. Leaving the explanation column blank means the complaint will not be examined.
Article invoked + Explanation (continued.case_law:
Dombo Beheer B.V. v. Netherlands  ECtHR, 27 October 1993
Under the equality of arms principle, each party must be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present their case without disadvantage vis-à-vis the opponent. Identify the specific procedural asymmetry and explain how it placed you at a substantial disadvantage.

Nideröst-Huber v. Switzerland  ECtHR, 18 February 1997
A violation was found where a document was placed before the court without being communicated to the parties. If materials before a court were never shown to you, identify this explicitly as a violation of the adversarial principle.

Krčmář and Others v. Czech Republic  ECtHR, 3 March 2000
The adversarial principle applies to substantive evidentiary materials used by a court to decide a case. If the court based its decision on evidence you were never shown or could not challenge, this is a strong Article 6(1) ground.

De Cubber v. Belgium  ECtHR, 26 October 1984
A judge who conducted the pre-trial investigation and then sat at trial creates an objective impartiality concern. Practical necessity does not justify the dual role. Identify the judge’s prior function and connection to the case explicitly.

Remedies used + Dates of final decisions.admissibility_guide:
Article 35 § 1: “The Court may only deal with the matter after all domestic remedies have been exhausted, according to the generally recognised rules of international law.”

The rule is applied with flexibility and without excessive formalism, but applicants must comply with domestic procedural rules and time-limits.

Burden of proof: Once the Government raises a non-exhaustion objection, the burden shifts to you to show that you exhausted available remedies, or that an exception applies.

Four-month rule: The period normally runs from the date of service of the final domestic decision. The Court applies this rule of its own motion.
Remedies used + Dates of final decisions.application_notes:
For each complaint in Part F, provide:

•	Exact date of the final decision.
•	Name of the court or tribunal and the nature of its decision.
•	Dates and outcomes of all lower court decisions.
•	The case file number in the domestic proceedings.

Example: Art. 6 complaint — Regional Court rejected claim 03/04/2015 (No. 1234/15); Court of Appeal dismissed appeal 12/09/2017; Supreme Court refused leave 08/03/2018.

Attach: All court decisions from every level; copies of your appeal submissions; proof of the date of service of the final decision.
Remedies used + Dates of final decisions.common_mistakes:
Mistake no. 8: Leaving the remedies section blank. The Court cannot verify the four-month time-limit without this information.

Mistake no. 4: Providing only the highest appeal decision. All decisions at every level must be submitted.
Remedies used + Dates of final decisions.case_law:
Vučković and Others v. Serbia  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 25 March 2014
Applicants must exhaust all effective domestic remedies, including newly established ones.

Akdivar and Others v. Turkey  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 16 September 1996
The exhaustion obligation does not apply where remedies are ineffective in practice or incapable of providing adequate redress. Where you claim an exception, explain specifically why.

Kudła v. Poland  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 26 October 2000
If a domestic length-of-proceedings remedy exists in the respondent State, it must be exhausted before coming to Strasbourg.

Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine  ECtHR (admissibility decision)
Sending an incomplete application does not stop the four-month clock. Failure to attach evidence of exhaustion at the time of lodging may mean the deadline passes before the omission is remedied.

Is or was there an appeal or remedy avai.admissibility_guide: This Yes/No declaration allows the Court to assess whether there are unused remedies that should have been exhausted. If you answer Yes, Field 65 must be completed. Answering No when a remedy was available risks inadmissibility if the Government later raises a non-exhaustion objection.
Is or was there an appeal or remedy avai.application_notes: Tick Yes or No. If Yes, complete Field 65. If No, leave Field 65 blank. Consider carefully: even a remedy you believe was ineffective should be disclosed here with the explanation in Field 65.
Is or was there an appeal or remedy avai.common_mistakes: Answering No without careful consideration can lead to inadmissibility for non-exhaustion at a later stage when the Government raises it. When in doubt, answer Yes and explain in Field 65.
Is or was there an appeal or remedy avai.case_law:
Akdivar and Others v. Turkey  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 16 September 1996
You are not required to exhaust every conceivable remedy, only those that are effective and accessible. If a remedy would have been purely theoretical or ineffective, answering No to Field 64 may be appropriate, but the factual basis must be clear from the application.

If you answered Yes above, please state.admissibility_guide:
You are not required to exhaust every conceivable remedy. The rule does not apply to remedies that are ineffective, unavailable, purely discretionary or extraordinary.

However, a bare assertion that a remedy was ineffective is not sufficient. You must show specifically why, in your particular circumstances, the remedy was not one you were required to use.
If you answered Yes above, please state.application_notes:
Identify the remedy you did not use and explain clearly why not. Acceptable reasons:

•	The remedy was not effective in your specific situation.
•	The remedy was purely discretionary or extraordinary (outside normal appeal procedures).
•	The remedy was not available at the relevant time.
•	There were specific legal or practical obstacles to using it.

Be specific: Simply saying a remedy was “ineffective” without explanation is insufficient.
If you answered Yes above, please state.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 8 (continued): If there were no effective remedies to exhaust, briefly explain this. Do not leave Field 65 blank without explanation if you answered Yes to Field 64.
If you answered Yes above, please state.case_law:
Akdivar and Others v. Turkey  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 16 September 1996
Where remedies are ineffective in practice, an applicant is not required to pursue them. This case supports Field 65 explanations where you assert a nominally available remedy would not have provided meaningful redress.

Vučković and Others v. Serbia  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 25 March 2014
The burden lies with the applicant to demonstrate that an available remedy was in fact ineffective or inaccessible. Refer to domestic case law or practice confirming the remedy’s ineffectiveness where possible.

Have you raised any of these complaints.admissibility_guide:
Article 35 § 2(b): The Court shall not deal with any application substantially the same as a matter submitted to another procedure of international investigation or settlement that contains no relevant new information.

The Court examines this of its own motion. Deliberately concealing parallel proceedings constitutes an abuse of the right of application.
Have you raised any of these complaints.application_notes: Tick Yes or No. If Yes, complete Field 67. If No, leave Field 67 blank. Answer honestly: concealing parallel proceedings is treated as misleading the Court.
Have you raised any of these complaints.common_mistakes: Answering No when parallel proceedings do or did exist is a serious error that can lead to the application being struck out as abusive.
Have you raised any of these complaints.case_law:
Z and Others v. United Kingdom  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 10 May 2001
The Court confirmed it will not examine applications substantially the same as a matter already before another international procedure. Even an inadmissibility decision by the earlier body does not automatically allow resubmission to the ECtHR.

If you answered Yes above, please give a.admissibility_guide:
Qualifying bodies include: UN human rights treaty bodies (Human Rights Committee, CEDAW Committee, ILO Committee, etc.) and international arbitration panels. The Court considers the body’s legal character, competence and founding instrument.

Transparency is essential: Deliberately concealing parallel proceedings constitutes an abuse of the right of application and will result in the application being struck out.
If you answered Yes above, please give a.application_notes:
Provide a concise summary including:

•	The name of the international body.
•	The date(s) you submitted your complaints.
•	Details of proceedings that took place.
•	The nature and date of any decisions.

Attach: Copies of relevant decisions and documents.

Example: Complaint submitted to the UN Human Rights Committee on 14/06/2020. The Committee declared it inadmissible on 10/02/2022. Copy attached.
If you answered Yes above, please give a.common_mistakes: Do not leave Field 67 blank if you answered Yes to Field 66. Incomplete or misleading disclosure of parallel proceedings is treated as misleading the Court.
If you answered Yes above, please give a.case_law:
Z and Others v. United Kingdom  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 10 May 2001
All parallel proceedings before other international bodies must be fully disclosed and relevant decisions attached.

Miroļubovs and Others v. Latvia  ECtHR, 15 September 2009
An application may be rejected as abusive where it is based on untrue facts or involves deliberate misleading of the Court. Failure to disclose parallel proceedings is such conduct.

Do you currently have, or have you previ.admissibility_guide: Previous applications must be disclosed because they may be substantially the same as the current application (leading to inadmissibility), and because the Court needs to manage related cases efficiently and may link them for examination.
Do you currently have, or have you previ.application_notes: Tick Yes or No. This covers both current pending applications and past ones. If Yes, provide the application number(s) in Field 69.
Do you currently have, or have you previ.common_mistakes: Failing to disclose previous applications — especially those raising the same or related complaints — risks the application being declared inadmissible as substantially the same matter.
Do you currently have, or have you previ.case_law:
Z and Others v. United Kingdom  ECtHR (Grand Chamber), 10 May 2001
The Court cannot deal with applications substantially the same as a matter it has already examined. Disclose all previous applications and explain how the current case raises new issues or contains relevant new information.

If you answered Yes above, please write.admissibility_guide: Application numbers allow the Court to trace previous files and assess whether the current application is genuinely new or a re-lodging of an already examined matter. Application numbers take the form XXXXX/YY (e.g. 12345/20).
If you answered Yes above, please write.application_notes: Write all application numbers assigned by the Court in previous cases. If you do not have the number, write the date and a brief description of the case so the Registry can locate it.
If you answered Yes above, please write.common_mistakes: Leaving Field 69 blank after answering Yes to Field 68 is an incomplete response. Provide all application numbers known to you.
If you answered Yes above, please write.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

List of all documents enclosed (chronolo.admissibility_guide:
All documents attached to the application must be listed. This enables the Court to find specific documents quickly when examining the case and to verify that the application is complete.

Documents not listed may be overlooked. Referring in the form to a document that is not in the bundle makes it impossible to verify the facts.
List of all documents enclosed (chronolo.application_notes:
You must enclose a numbered, chronological list of ALL documents attached.

•	Arrange documents in chronological order and by set of proceedings.
•	Number all pages of all documents consecutively throughout the bundle.
•	Indicate in the list the page number where each document can be found.
•	Do NOT staple, bind or tape the documents.
•	Submit copies only — originals will not be returned.
•	If the space on the form is insufficient, continue on an attached sheet.

Documents to include: all court decisions at every level; copies of your appeals; medical reports, witness statements, transcripts; proof of service of the final decision.
List of all documents enclosed (chronolo.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 10: Forgetting to put a list of documents on the form. The Court must be able to find particular documents when examining the case. Listing with page numbers in chronological order is essential for the efficient processing of incoming applications.
List of all documents enclosed (chronolo.case_law:
Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine  ECtHR (admissibility decision)
Applications were rejected because the bundle was incomplete: domestic decisions and appeal documents were missing. Without a complete and indexed bundle, the Court cannot verify exhaustion of remedies or the four-month time-limit.

Any other comments about your applicatio.admissibility_guide: Field 71 is not a substitute for the substantive sections E, F or G. The Court will not accept comments here as a replacement for facts, violations or remedies information that should appear in their respective sections.
Any other comments about your applicatio.application_notes: Optional. Use this field only to flag something that does not fit elsewhere: a request for anonymity, a note about urgency, an explanation of why a document was unobtainable, or a cross-reference to related proceedings not covered in Part H.
Any other comments about your applicatio.common_mistakes: Do not use Field 71 to summarise your facts or complaints. Each section (E, F, G) must be properly completed in the spaces provided.
Any other comments about your applicatio.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Date of declaration.admissibility_guide: The date of the declaration confirms when the form was finalised. It should correspond to the date of dispatch. The four-month deadline is calculated from the postmark, but significant discrepancies between the signature date and the dispatch date may prompt Registry queries.
Date of declaration.application_notes: Format: DD/MM/YYYY. Sign and date the form on or close to the day it is sent. Do not pre-date or post-date.
Date of declaration.common_mistakes: Mistake no. 12: Sending the form at the last moment. Sign and date the form as close as possible to the day of dispatch to avoid any discrepancy.
Date of declaration.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.

Signature(s) — Applicant(s) or Represent.admissibility_guide: The declaration is a mandatory formal requirement under Rule 47. The form is a legal document with legal consequences. Without an original signature, the application is not valid and will not be registered.
Signature(s) — Applicant(s) or Represent.application_notes:
Original signature required. Tick whether signing as Applicant(s) or Representative(s).

If there are multiple applicants and no representative, each applicant signs. If represented, the representative signs.

This is the final declaration that all information in the form is accurate to the best of your knowledge and belief.
Signature(s) — Applicant(s) or Represent.common_mistakes:
Mistake no. 5: Not sending the form with the original signature. Photocopies, scans or faxes are not accepted. The physically signed paper form must be sent by post.

Mistake no. 13: Re-submitting an incomplete application. If your first application was rejected, no file is kept. You must download a fresh form, complete it in full and attach all documents — even those sent previously.
Signature(s) — Applicant(s) or Represent.case_law:
Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine  ECtHR (admissibility decision)
The application is only registered — and the four-month period only interrupted — when a complete application, including a properly signed declaration, is received by the Court.

Miroļubovs and Others v. Latvia  ECtHR, 15 September 2009
By signing Field 73, the applicant declares that the information is accurate and submitted in good faith. Deliberately providing false information or concealing parallel proceedings constitutes an abuse of the right of application.

Confirmation of correspondent — name and.admissibility_guide: The Court corresponds with only one applicant or one representative. This ensures communications are not duplicated, lost or contradicted. The correspondent identified in Field 74 becomes the Court’s single point of contact for all subsequent correspondence.
Confirmation of correspondent — name and.application_notes:
Required where there are multiple applicants or multiple representatives.

•	Multiple applicants, no representative: identify one applicant as the correspondent.
•	If represented: the Registry corresponds only with the representative. Identify the lawyer or other representative.
•	Multiple lawyers: identify which lawyer will conduct correspondence with the Court.
•	Tick the appropriate box: Applicant or Representative.
Confirmation of correspondent — name and.common_mistakes: Failing to complete Field 74 where there are multiple applicants or representatives creates confusion about who the Court should contact. Missed correspondence can lead to the file being treated as inactive.
Confirmation of correspondent — name and.case_law: No specific ECtHR judgment addresses this administrative field directly. The general principle from Malysh and Ivanin v. Ukraine applies: any incompleteness in the application form means the application will not be registered and the four-month clock will not stop. Every applicable field must be correctly completed.
